skill.name: resource-monitor
skill.description: Monitor CPU, memory, disk, network, and process metrics with command examples and simple Node.js or Python scripts.
skill.summary: Guide agents through local resource monitoring, alert thresholds, and capacity recommendations.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many external command, network, filesystem, obfuscation, and blocker patterns in SKILL.md. AI review found no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection, but the skill does recommend local system, process, filesystem, and network inspection commands that can expose sensitive host metadata.

medium_findings.title: Local System Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends running shell commands such as top, ps, df, iostat, du, ncdu, netstat, ss, iftop, and nload. These are legitimate monitoring commands, but they can reveal process lists, filesystem layout, disk usage, and network connection metadata on the host.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and semantically tied to monitoring. They are not malicious by themselves, but they inspect sensitive local host state.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem and Process Metadata Collection
medium_findings.description: The examples read Linux system metadata and collect disk, memory, process, and network counters. This supports the advertised purpose, but users should approve collection scope before using it on shared or production systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly access system resource data through /proc, filesystem tools, and psutil. The context is operational monitoring, so the concern is data exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Network Enumeration Commands
medium_findings.description: The network section recommends commands that list active connections and connection states. This can be useful for troubleshooting, but it may disclose service names, listening ports, peer addresses, and traffic patterns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The netstat, ss, iftop, and nload examples are clear network inspection guidance. The report IP addresses are private sample data, so this is a moderate operational privacy risk, not exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Obfuscation Matches Are ANSI Color Escapes
low_findings.description: The hex and escape-sequence matches are used for terminal colors in progress bars. I did not find evidence that these strings decode or hide executable payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The escape values are immediately commented or used as terminal color codes. Their surrounding logic only formats local console output.

low_findings.title: Scheduling and Weak Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner matched words such as at in timestamps and report prose, not at-command scheduling. I did not find evidence of cryptographic operations or weak hash usage in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text and sample timestamps. There is no command scheduling syntax or crypto implementation in context.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Monitoring Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple Markdown examples instruct users to run host inspection commands. Treat these as permission-sensitive operations on production or multi-user systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and executable if copied by an agent or user. Their purpose is legitimate monitoring, which limits severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Long-Running Monitoring Loops
dangerous_patterns.description: The Node.js and Python examples run repeated monitoring loops. These can consume terminal sessions and collect continuous host metrics until stopped.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loops are explicit in the sample code. They do not send data externally, but they can run indefinitely and should be user-controlled.

content.value_statement: Resource issues are hard to diagnose when CPU, memory, disk, and network data are scattered. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured monitoring commands, scripts, reports, and threshold guidance.
actual_capabilities: Lists commands for CPU, memory, disk, network, and process inspection.
Provides Node.js and Python examples for periodic local resource logging.
Defines alert thresholds for CPU, memory, disk, and network conditions.
Shows a structured resource monitoring report format for operational reviews.
Suggests optimization, scaling, and capacity planning actions from metrics.
limitations: Does not install or configure monitoring agents by itself.
Requires host permissions for some process, disk, and network commands.
Examples may need changes for non-Linux systems or restricted containers.
Does not provide secure storage or retention controls for collected metrics.

use_cases.title: Investigate a Slow Service
use_cases.description: Collect CPU, memory, disk, and connection data before deciding whether code, database, or infrastructure changes are needed.

use_cases.title: Prepare Production Health Reports
use_cases.description: Turn daily resource metrics into a readable report with warnings, trends, and recommended actions.

use_cases.title: Plan Capacity Upgrades
use_cases.description: Review utilization patterns and growth trends to decide when storage, memory, or compute capacity should change.

prompt_templates.title: Check Current Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the resource-monitor skill to list safe commands for checking CPU, memory, disk, and network usage on this machine.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Bottleneck
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the resource-monitor skill to help me identify whether CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network activity is causing this slowdown.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Alert Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the resource-monitor skill to design warning and critical thresholds for a production web service.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Capacity Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the resource-monitor skill to turn these resource measurements into a capacity planning report with risks and next actions.

output_examples.input: Review a server where CPU peaks at 87 percent and memory peaks at 85 percent during report generation.
output_examples.output: A concise health report that marks CPU and memory as warnings, links the spike to report generation, and recommends off-peak scheduling plus streaming or pagination.

output_examples.input: Find why disk usage is rising on a production host.
output_examples.output: A disk-focused analysis that ranks large directories, estimates growth rate, flags log growth, and recommends log rotation and archiving.

output_examples.input: Create monitoring thresholds for a new web service.
output_examples.output: A threshold plan with warning and critical levels for CPU, memory, disk, and network errors, plus guidance to avoid excessive alerts.

best_practices: Ask for approval before running commands that expose process, filesystem, or network details.
Collect a short baseline before changing infrastructure or application settings.
Correlate resource spikes with deployments, jobs, traffic changes, and database activity.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad host inspection commands on systems you are not authorized to monitor.
Do not treat one high metric as root cause without checking related signals.
Do not store raw monitoring output with secrets, private paths, or internal IP details unnecessarily.

faq.question: Does this skill run monitoring commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that an agent or user may choose to run with permission.

faq.question: Which resources can it help monitor?
faq.answer: It covers CPU, memory, disk space, disk I/O, network traffic, connections, and process-level metrics.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can be used in production with approval, scoped commands, and care around sensitive host metadata.

faq.question: Does it replace Prometheus or Datadog?
faq.answer: No. It helps plan checks, scripts, reports, and thresholds, but it is not a full monitoring platform.

faq.question: Can it detect memory leaks?
faq.answer: It can guide memory checks and trend review, but confirming a leak needs repeated measurements and application context.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: Basic checks may run as a normal user, while some process, disk, and network tools may require elevated permissions.
